vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's dcredit parameter controls requirements for usage of digits in a password. When set to a negative number, any password will be required to contain that many digits. When set to a positive number, pam_pwquality will grant +1 additional length credit for each digit. Modify the dcredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the use of a digit in passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_dcredit='' if grep -sq dcredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/dcredit/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^dcredit") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_dcredit" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -

triage_decision.reason: Changing the PAM dcredit setting to enforce at least one digit is a standard password policy hardening step that does not affect system availability or critical services. It is a simple configuration edit with minimal risk of lockout if applied correctly.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user lockout if passwords do not meet new digit requirement

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will correctly set dcredit to -1 in all relevant pwquality configuration files, thereby enforcing the required digit rule and addressing the vulnerability. It is straightforward, does not affect other services, and avoids lockout risks if applied correctly.
concerns: The sed pattern replaces only the first occurrence per file; if multiple entries exist, additional lines may remain unchanged. The plan does not backup the original files before modification, which could make recovery harder if a mistake occurs. The approach of checking for replacement and then appending may be slightly fragile if the file has unusual formatting or multiple commented/uncommented lines.
The use of read_file before sed is unnecessary for the actual change and could introduce race conditions if the file changes between read and write.
suggested_improvements: Use a single sed invocation with the -e option to ensure all matching lines (including multiple clashing dcredit entries) are updated, e.g., `sed -i -e 's/^\s*#\s*dcredit\s*=.*$/dcredit = -1/' -e 's/^\s*dcredit\s*=.*$/dcredit = -1/'` and then optionally add the line if not present using grep -q.
Create a backup of each config file before modification (e.g., `cp -p /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak`).
Consider using `pwquality`’s built-in searchpath ordering and limiting changes to the main config file unless overridden by an include file is explicitly required.
Add proper error handling: check exit status of sed and ensure the file is actually writable (root privileges).
